🎯 Key points for quick navigation:

💣 Opening Range Breakout (ORB) trading is highly popular but often leads to rapid account failure for those who don't understand its inherent traps.
📦 The "ORB box" is defined by marking the high and low of the first 15 minutes of the trading session.
⚠️ Classic ORB trading fails because it ignores the higher time frame context and existing price action to the left of the opening range.
📉 Many traders get stopped out because they enter on a breakout that is actually just a "liquidity sweep" before price reverses.
🎭 False breakouts are a primary reason for failure; price pokes above the range to "suck traders in" before slamming in the opposite direction.
💧 Inexperienced traders often unknowingly "become the liquidity" for smarter institutional players by entering immediately on a 5-minute candle close.
🏗️ Entering too early without structure confirmation results in "guessing" rather than trading, as the market has not yet established a definitive direction.
🎰 Overtrading and chasing every single breakout leads to "getting chopped to pieces" and giving back all previous gains.
🔄 To avoid false signals, traders must wait for a specific five-minute chart breakout rather than just a simple close above the 15-minute range level.
🕯️ A critical requirement is an "overlapping Fair Value Gap" (FVG) where the middle candle of a three-candle displacement pattern breaks the range boundary.
📈 This specific displacement serves as proof that buyers (or sellers) are truly in control, distinguishing a genuine trend from a random price push.
⏳ Patience is required for step three: waiting for the market to retest the high or low of the initial candle where the Fair Value Gap overlaps.
🔥 The actual entry signal is an "engulfing candle" during the retest; this confirmation ensures the breakout is real and ready to run.
🎯 Risk management involves placing the stop-loss at the recent low and targeting a clean 2:1 Reward-to-Risk (2R) ratio once the engulfing candle closes.
📉 Standard ORB strategies often fail because traders enter the moment a 5-minute candle closes outside the range without any further validation.
🚫 "Full porting" or over-leveraging on a simple breakout frequently leads to accounts being blown when price action "melts" against the position.
🔍 Amateur traders often get trapped by "looking left" and spotting a major swing point, mistakenly thinking they have identified the correct market direction after a liquidity sweep.
📉 Waiting for a simple body closure below the range low to enter a trade often leads to immediate stop-outs, proving that standard candle breaks are not profitable signals.
🛑 A key rule for consistency and capital preservation is to stop trading for the day after taking two losses; revenge trading is a trap.
📏 The high-probability setup requires a "displacement" through the range level that creates a visible Fair Value Gap (FVG) where the first and third candles' shadows do not overlap.
🧱 A valid entry must involve more than just hitting the FVG; the market must specifically retrace to and reject the initial range high or low.
🕯️ Confirmation comes only from a specific "engulfing candlestick"—a candle that closes completely above (for longs) or below (for shorts) the previous opposing candle.
❌ If price action runs and takes out the opposite side of the range before your entry criteria are met, the original trade idea is officially invalidated.
🛡️ The beauty of this refined method is that it keeps you out of the market when criteria aren't met, saving you from losses where standard ORB traders would have been stopped out.
🧘 Missing a trade is better than taking a loss; "sitting on your hands" when entry criteria aren't perfectly met is a hallmark of a profitable trader.
📈 On the next trading day, look for displacement and a Fair Value Gap (FVG) immediately upon the 15-minute candle printing to establish a bullish or bearish bias.
📦 After identifying the 15-minute range, drop to the 5-minute chart and wait for a breakout that specifically creates a new FVG.
🛡️ Entry requires a specific sequence: price must trade back into the FVG and touch the range high/low, followed by an engulfing candle that forms a "valid order block."
🎯 Set the stop-loss at the swing low to protect against raids; if price returns to that level, the trade idea is invalidated and you should exit immediately.
🏃 Target a 2:1 Reward-to-Risk ratio for the initial exit, then secure partial profits and leave a small "runner" for potential higher time frame moves.
🗺️ High win rates require top-down analysis, starting with the Daily time frame to identify the overall trend (e.g., higher highs and higher lows).
🔄 Market cycles move from "external range liquidity" (sweeping highs/lows) back to "internal range liquidity" (filling Fair Value Gaps).
⏳ Price delivery follows a predictable phase: it moves from expansion to either consolidation or reversal before expanding again.
🔄 A sweep of a previous swing high followed by untapped internal liquidity indicates a high probability of an expansion-to-reversal phase.
📉 Failing to displace above a high on the 1-hour chart signals a bearish shift, making lower liquidity pools the "low-hanging fruit" for targets.
🧠 Establishing a clear directional bias (bullish or bearish) before the session begins is essential for filtering out low-probability ORB setups.
🛡️ Observing the market reject a Fair Value Gap or form a "propulsion block" on the 15-minute chart provides the institutional "footprints" needed for a high-probability trade.
🎣 The 9:30 candle often creates a manipulation move (sweeping the high) before distributing lower, aligning perfectly with a bearish top-down analysis.
❌ Entering immediately on a 5-minute break remains the most common way traders get stopped out by minor retracements.
🕯️ By waiting for the market to trade back into the range level and provide an engulfing candle, you confirm the move's validity and improve entry precision.
🏆 Combining the refined ORB entry with daily targets (like a Daily Fair Value Gap) can transform a standard 2R trade into a massive 6R+ runner.

The ORB strategy is very profitable, but, as he stated in the video, you have to use it correctly. I’ve moved from the 5min to 10min TF and you get a lot less of those fake breakouts of the high and low of the ORB. Also, when a 10min candle breaks the high or low and closes outside of it, you typically see the follow through. You won’t always get an FVG retest or engulfing candle because of the higher TF, but you don’t always need it.

One other comment, basically what others have said, it just so happens I have been experimenting show;g FVG on my ORB charts, and I too observed that FVG really don’t happen often on QQQ, or other securities.  So instead of this strategy requiring a FVG, I think it should be a conditional adjustment to the ORB.  That is “ if you see a FVG, THEN, do this”.  Also as some have  said, if you have a rocket ship that just blasts away from the open at Warp 9, you wont get any of this and you will miss a 10 bagger that could have made bank for a week of ORBs.  That is not a critique of this specific strategy or video, but rather a general critique of ORB.  That is, how do you break lock from the ORB when a true ripper starts that without ORB you would not have missed.  There needs to be a way to determine that today is not an ORB day but rather either a nothing day or a ripper day.  Maybe I’m asking for too much.

14:34 where is the engulfing candle
This was most realistic version video of this trade

ORB Rules (This video)
1. Mark the high and low of the first 15-minute candle after the 9:30 AM New York session open to form the ORB box. 
2. Switch to the 5-minute chart and wait for a candle to break and close above (for longs) or below (for shorts) the ORB box, forming an overlapping Fair Value Gap (FVG)—a 3-candle pattern where the first candle's high and third candle's low don't overlap. 
3. Wait for price to retest the broken ORB high/low level, entering the gap area. 
4. Enter on the engulfing candle that fully engulfs the retest candle (bullish engulfing for longs, bearish for shorts).
 5. Place stop loss below the retest candle low (longs) or above the high (shorts); target at least 2R (risk-to-reward). 
6. Limit to 1-2 trades per day; sit out after losses to avoid overtrading.
